preamble.summary: Proverbs 23 continues the 'Words of the Wise' collection with extended instructions on self-control in the presence of power, the futility of chasing wealth, respect for parents and the aged, avoidance of prostitution, and a vivid concluding portrait of the drunkard. The chapter moves from table manners before a ruler to the devastating consequences of alcohol abuse.
preamble.remarkable: The warning against staring at wealth (vv4-5) contains one of the most striking images in Proverbs: riches sprout wings and fly away like an eagle toward the sky. The Hebrew makes the eagle's flight a spontaneous generation — wealth 'makes itself wings,' as if it were alive and determined to escape. The chapter's final section on drunkenness (vv29-35) is the longest sustained poetic treatment of alcohol in the Hebrew Bible. It is not a moral lecture but a masterful piece of observational writing — the drunkard's bloodshot eyes, unexplained wounds, swaying vision, and the devastating final line: 'When will I wake up? I need another drink.' The addict's own voice closes the poem.
preamble.friction: Verse 13-14 endorse corporal punishment of children with the claim that beating will not kill the child and may save him from Sheol. Modern readers must reckon with the cultural distance: physical discipline was assumed across the ancient Near East, and the proverb operates within that assumption. The text is not a license for abuse but a reflection of ancient pedagogy. The 'stingy host' passage (vv6-8) uses the phrase ra-ayin ('evil eye') which in this context means 'grudging, miserly' — not the supernatural 'evil eye' of later folk tradition.
preamble.connections: The 'Words of the Wise' section continues from 22:17. The wealth-sprouting-wings image (v5) connects to the transience theme in Ecclesiastes. The 'do not move a boundary marker' (v10) repeats 22:28 and Deuteronomy 19:14. The father-rejoicing theme (vv15-16, 24-25) echoes Proverbs 10:1 and 15:20. The drunkard portrait (vv29-35) parallels Isaiah 28:7-8 and anticipates Habakkuk 2:15-16.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־תֵ֭שֵׁב לִלְח֣וֹם אֶת־מוֹשֵׁ֑ל בִּ֥ין תָּ֝בִ֗ין אֶת־אֲשֶׁ֥ר לְפָנֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: When thou sittest to eat with a ruler, consider diligently what is before thee:
verses.rendering:
When you sit down to eat with a ruler,
pay careful attention to what is before you.
translator_notes: Bin tavin ('understand carefully, discern thoroughly') is an emphatic form — the doubled verb insists on maximum awareness. Dining with a ruler was a political act in the ancient world, not a casual meal. 'What is before you' has a double meaning: the food on the table and the powerful person across from you. Both require vigilance.

verses.text_hebrew: וְשַׂמְתָּ֣ שַׂכִּ֣ין בְּלֹעֶ֑ךָ אִ֝֗ם בַּ֣עַל נֶ֣פֶשׁ אָֽתָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: And put a knife to thy throat, if thou be a man given to appetite.
verses.rendering:
Put a knife to your throat
if you are a person with a big appetite.
translator_notes: The image is deliberately shocking: the knife at the throat is a hyperbolic metaphor for extreme self-control. If your appetite controls you, you must take drastic measures to control it before it controls the situation. Ba'al nefesh ('master of appetite, person of great desire') describes someone whose hunger — for food, for status, for the ruler's favor — could lead him to overindulge and make a fool of himself.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תִּ֭תְאָו לְמַטְעַמֹּתָ֑יו וְ֝ה֗וּא לֶ֣חֶם כְּזָבִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Be not desirous of his dainties: for they are deceitful meat.
verses.rendering:
Do not crave his delicacies,
for they are deceptive food.
translator_notes: Lechem kezavim ('bread of lies, deceptive food') warns that the ruler's table is not generous hospitality but a calculated display. The food comes with strings attached. Every bite accepted creates an obligation. The delicacies (mat'ammotav) are bait, not gifts.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תִּיגַ֥ע לְֽהַעֲשִׁ֑יר מִֽבִּינָ֥תְךָ֗ חֲדָֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: Labour not to be rich: cease from thine own wisdom.
verses.rendering:
Do not exhaust yourself chasing wealth;
have the sense to stop.
translator_notes: Tiga ('to toil, to weary yourself, to exhaust') implies not ordinary work but obsessive, health-destroying labor driven by greed. The command mi-binatekha chadal ('from your understanding, stop') means: use the very intelligence you are devoting to wealth-getting to recognize when to quit. Wisdom tells you where ambition should end.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲתָ֤עִ֥יף עֵינֶ֗יךָ בּ֭וֹ וְאֵינֶ֑נּוּ כִּ֤י עָשֹׂ֣ה יַעֲשֶׂה־לּ֭וֹ כְנָפַ֗יִם כְּנֶ֣שֶׁר יעוף [וָעָ֣ף] הַשָּׁמָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: Wilt thou set thine eyes upon that which is not? for riches certainly make themselves wings; they fly away as an eagle toward heaven.
verses.rendering:
Will you fix your gaze on what is already gone?
Wealth sprouts wings for itself
and flies off like an eagle toward the sky.
verses.expanded_rendering: The image is unforgettable: the moment you stare at wealth (ha-ta'if einekha, 'do you cause your eyes to fly toward it?'), it vanishes. It does not merely disappear — it grows wings and escapes under its own power, like an eagle ascending beyond reach. The verb asoh ya'aseh-lo ('it certainly makes for itself') gives wealth agency: money does not passively shrink; it actively escapes. The eagle (nesher) is the most powerful bird in the Israelite sky — once it lifts off, nothing can bring it back. The theological point is clear: wealth is not stable enough to bear the weight of human hope.
translator_notes: The wordplay between ha-ta'if ('will you cause your eyes to fly') and ya'uf ('it flies away') connects the straining eye to the departing wealth — your eyes fly toward it, and it flies away from you.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תִּלְחַ֗ם אֶת־לֶ֭חֶם רַ֣ע עָ֑יִן וְאַל־תתאו [תִּ֝תְאָ֗יו] לְמַטְעַמֹּתָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: Eat thou not the bread of him that hath an evil eye, neither desire thou his dainty meats:
verses.rendering:
Do not eat the bread of a stingy host,
and do not crave his delicacies.
translator_notes: Ra ayin ('evil of eye') is the opposite of tov-ayin ('good of eye,' 22:9). The 'evil eye' in Hebrew wisdom means 'grudging, miserly, resentful of what others receive' — not the supernatural curse of later folklore. Eating a stingy person's food creates obligation to someone who will later resent having shared it.

key_terms.semantic_range: evil of eye, grudging, miserly, envious; one whose perspective is oriented toward withholding
key_terms.note: The idiom describes a disposition, not a supernatural power. A ra-ayin person counts every morsel he shares and holds it against you. His generosity is performance, not genuine.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י כְמוֹ־שָׁעַ֥ר בְּנַפְשׁ֗וֹ כֶּ֫ן ה֥וּא אֱכֹ֣ל וּ֭שְׁתֵה יֹ֣אמַר לָ֑ךְ וְ֝לִבּ֗וֹ בַּל־עִמָּֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: For as he thinketh in his heart, so is he: Eat and drink, saith he to thee; but his heart is not with thee.
verses.rendering:
For as he calculates inside himself, so he is.
'Eat! Drink!' he says to you,
but his heart is not with you.
verses.expanded_rendering: The famous phrase 'as he thinks in his heart, so he is' is often quoted out of context as positive-thinking philosophy. In context, it describes the stingy host: his true self is the inner calculator (kemo sha'ar be-nafsho, 'like one who reckons within himself'), not the outward display of hospitality. The verb sha'ar may mean 'to reckon, to calculate, to estimate' — he is pricing every bite you take. His mouth says 'eat and drink' (social obligation demands it), but his lev ('heart, mind, inner self') is bal immakh ('not with you') — he resents your presence at his table.
translator_notes: The rendering of sha'ar is debated — it may mean 'gatekeeper' (one who guards the threshold) or 'calculator' (one who reckons costs). Both meanings fit: the stingy host is a gatekeeper of his own generosity, calculating what everything costs him.

verses.text_hebrew: פִּֽתְּךָ֣ אָ֭כַלְתָּ תְקִיאֶ֑נָּה וְ֝שִׁחַ֗תָּ דְּבָרֶ֥יךָ הַנְּעִימִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The morsel which thou hast eaten shalt thou vomit up, and lose thy sweet words.
verses.rendering:
The little you have eaten you will vomit up,
and your pleasant words will be wasted.
translator_notes: The physical disgust of vomiting (teqi'ennah) matches the social disgust of wasted courtesy. When you discover the host's true nature, everything — the food and your compliments — will come back up. The proverb warns that accepting hospitality from the wrong person degrades both body and speech.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּאָזְנֵ֣י כְ֭סִיל אַל־תְּדַבֵּ֑ר כִּֽי־יָ֝ב֗וּז לְשֵׂ֣כֶל מִלֶּֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Speak not in the ears of a fool: for he will despise the wisdom of thy words.
verses.rendering:
Do not speak in the hearing of a fool,
for he will despise the insight of your words.
translator_notes: The kesil ('fool, dullard') is not unintelligent but uninterested. He yavuz ('despises, treats with contempt') the sekhel ('insight, good sense') of your words. Teaching a fool is not merely ineffective — it is self-degrading, because your wisdom is subjected to contempt.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תַּ֭סֵּג גְּב֣וּל עוֹלָ֑ם וּבִשְׂדֵ֥י יְ֝תוֹמִ֗ים אַל־תָּבֹֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: Remove not the old landmark; and enter not into the fields of the fatherless:
verses.rendering:
Do not move an ancient boundary marker,
and do not encroach on the fields of orphans,
translator_notes: This repeats the boundary-stone prohibition from 22:28 but adds a specific victim: orphans (yetomim). Orphans had no father to defend their property rights in court. Stealing their land was attacking the most defenseless members of society — a direct affront to God, who claims the role of orphan-defender (v11).

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־גֹאֲלָ֥ם חָזָ֑ק הֽוּא־יָרִ֖יב אֶת־רִיבָ֣ם אִתָּֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: For their redeemer is mighty; he shall plead their cause with thee.
verses.rendering:
because their Redeemer is strong;
He will take up their case against you.
verses.expanded_rendering: The go'el ('redeemer, kinsman-avenger') was the family member obligated to defend a relative's rights — to buy back sold land, to avenge murder, to rescue from slavery. Orphans have no human go'el, so God assumes the role. The adjective chazaq ('strong, mighty, powerful') assures that this Redeemer cannot be outmatched in court or in force. The verse transforms a property-rights warning into a theological declaration: God personally advocates for the fatherless, and anyone who steals from them faces a divine lawsuit.
translator_notes: Go'el is the same word used for God's redemption of Israel from Egypt (Exodus 6:6) and for Boaz's role in the book of Ruth. Here it names God's active, personal commitment to orphan defense.

key_terms.semantic_range: redeemer, kinsman-avenger, next-of-kin protector; one obligated to rescue and restore a family member's rights
key_terms.note: The go'el institution required the nearest relative to act: buy back land, free from slavery, avenge blood. When God claims this title, He claims family obligation toward the orphan.

verses.text_hebrew: הָבִ֣יאָה לַמּוּסָ֣ר לִבֶּ֑ךָ וְ֝אָזְנֶ֗ךָ לְאִמְרֵי־דָֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: Apply thine heart unto instruction, and thine ears to the words of knowledge.
verses.rendering:
Bring your heart to discipline
and your ears to words of knowledge.
translator_notes: Havi'ah ('bring, cause to come') is active — the student must deliver his own heart and ears to the learning process. Wisdom does not come to the passive; the student must come to wisdom. Musar ('discipline, correction, instruction') and imre-da'at ('words of knowledge') represent the full curriculum: correction of wrong behavior and acquisition of right understanding.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תִּמְנַ֣ע מִנַּ֣עַר מוּסָ֑ר כִּֽי־תַכֶּ֥נּוּ בַ֝שֵּׁ֗בֶט לֹ֣א יָמֽוּת׃
verses.text_kjv: Withhold not correction from the child: for if thou beatest him with the rod, he shall not die.
verses.rendering:
Do not withhold discipline from a youth;
if you strike him with the rod, he will not die.
translator_notes: The proverb operates within the universal ancient assumption that physical correction was part of child-rearing. The assurance 'he will not die' addresses a parent's hesitation — the rod causes temporary pain, not permanent harm. Modern readers must read this within its cultural context while recognizing that the underlying principle (correction is an act of love, not neglect) transcends the specific method.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֭תָּה בַּשֵּׁ֣בֶט תַּכֶּ֑נּוּ וְ֝נַפְשׁ֗וֹ מִשְּׁא֥וֹל תַּצִּֽיל׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou shalt beat him with the rod, and shalt deliver his soul from hell.
verses.rendering:
You will strike him with the rod
and rescue his life from Sheol.
translator_notes: Sheol (she'ol) is the underworld, the realm of the dead — not the later theological concept of hell. The proverb claims that corrective discipline can save a young person from the path that leads to early death. 'Rescue his nefesh from Sheol' means: keep him alive by keeping him on the right path. The stakes are existential, not merely behavioral.

key_terms.semantic_range: the underworld, the grave, the realm of the dead; the place where all the dead descend
key_terms.note: Sheol in the Hebrew Bible is not a place of punishment but the default destination of all the dead — a shadowy, diminished existence. Here it represents premature death resulting from an undisciplined life.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּ֭נִי אִם־חָכַ֣ם לִבֶּ֑ךָ יִשְׂמַ֖ח לִבִּ֣י גַם־אָֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: My son, if thine heart be wise, my heart shall rejoice, even mine.
verses.rendering:
My son, if your heart is wise,
my own heart will rejoice —
translator_notes: The teacher shifts to the intimate address 'my son' (beni) and reveals personal investment: the student's wisdom produces joy in the teacher's heart. Gam ani ('even I, I also, I myself') is emphatic — the teacher's delight is real, not professional. Wisdom is relational; it produces joy in the community of those who share it.

verses.text_hebrew: וְתַעְלֹ֥זְנָה כִלְיוֹתָ֑י בְּדַבֵּ֥ר שְׂ֝פָתֶ֗יךָ מֵישָׁרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, my reins shall rejoice, when thy lips speak right things.
verses.rendering:
yes, my inmost being will celebrate
when your lips speak what is right.
translator_notes: Kilyotai ('my kidneys, my innermost parts') represents the deepest seat of emotion in Hebrew anthropology — what we would call 'gut feeling' or 'the core of my being.' The teacher's joy at hearing the student speak mesharim ('upright things, straight words, what is right') is visceral, not cerebral.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־יְקַנֵּ֣א לִ֭בְּךָ בַּֽחַטָּאִ֑ים כִּ֥י אִם־בְּיִרְאַ֖ת יְהוָ֣ה כָּל־הַיּֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: Let not thine heart envy sinners: but be thou in the fear of the LORD all the day long.
verses.rendering:
Do not let your heart envy sinners,
but live in the fear of the LORD all day long.
translator_notes: The temptation to envy sinners (yeqanne libbekha ba-chatta'im) assumes that sinners appear to prosper — a problem that tormented the psalmists (Psalm 37, 73) and the author of Ecclesiastes. The antidote is not denial but redirection: yir'at YHWH ('the fear of the LORD') all day long. Sustained reverence displaces envy by reorienting what the heart values.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֥י אִם־יֵ֣שׁ אַחֲרִ֑ית וְ֝תִקְוָתְךָ֗ לֹ֣א תִכָּרֵֽת׃
verses.text_kjv: For surely there is an end; and thine expectation shall not be cut off.
verses.rendering:
For there is surely a future,
and your hope will not be cut off.
translator_notes: Acharit ('end, future, outcome, what comes after') assures the student that the story is not over. The apparent prosperity of sinners is not the final chapter. Tiqvatekha ('your hope, your expectation') will not be cut off (tikkaret, from karat, the same verb used for being 'cut off' from the covenant community). The promise is that the righteous path leads somewhere — patience will be vindicated.

key_terms.semantic_range: end, latter part, future, outcome; what comes after the present situation
key_terms.note: Acharit does not mean 'afterlife' in this context but 'outcome' — the eventual resolution of things. The wisdom tradition trusts that God's moral order will assert itself in time.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׁמַע־אַתָּ֣ה בְנִ֣י וַחֲכָ֑ם וְאַשֵּׁ֖ר בַּדֶּ֣רֶךְ לִבֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear thou, my son, and be wise, and guide thine heart in the way.
verses.rendering:
Listen, my son, and be wise,
and guide your heart on the right path.
translator_notes: The imperative sequence — shema ('listen'), chakkam ('be wise'), asher ('guide, set straight') — maps the learning process: attention leads to wisdom, which leads to directing the heart. The student is not passive but active: he must steer (asher) his own heart (lev) along the path (derekh). Wisdom is not received; it is practiced.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תְּהִ֥י בְסֹֽבְאֵי־יָ֑יִן בְּזֹלֲלֵ֖י בָשָׂ֣ר לָֽמוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Be not among winebibbers; among riotous eaters of flesh:
verses.rendering:
Do not be among heavy drinkers
or among those who gorge on meat.
translator_notes: Sov'e-yayin ('drunkards, wine-guzzlers') and zolale vasar ('gluttons of meat, flesh-devourers') represent excess in both drink and food. The prohibition is social: al-tehi ('do not be among, do not associate with') warns against the company of the intemperate. The issue is not wine or meat per se but the loss of self-control that comes from habitual overindulgence and the peer pressure that perpetuates it.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־סֹבֵ֣א וְ֭זוֹלֵל יִוָּרֵ֑שׁ וּ֝קְרָעִ֗ים תַּלְבִּ֥ישׁ נוּמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: For the drunkard and the glutton shall come to poverty: and drowsiness shall clothe a man with rags.
verses.rendering:
For the drunkard and the glutton will become poor,
and drowsiness dresses you in rags.
translator_notes: The three-step progression: excess leads to drowsiness (numah, 'sleepiness, slumber'), which leads to poverty, which leads to rags. The personification of drowsiness as a tailor who dresses you in torn clothing (qera'im, 'rags, torn garments') is darkly humorous — the habits you indulge in become the wardrobe you wear.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׁמַ֣ע לְ֭אָבִיךָ זֶ֣ה יְלָדֶ֑ךָ וְאַל־תָּ֝ב֗וּז כִּֽי־זָקְנָ֥ה אִמֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Hearken unto thy father that begat thee, and despise not thy mother when she is old.
verses.rendering:
Listen to your father, who gave you life,
and do not despise your mother when she is old.
translator_notes: Zeh yeladekha ('this one who fathered you, who gave you birth') grounds the command in biological reality: the person you are tempted to dismiss gave you existence. The second line addresses a specific temptation — contempt for an aging mother (al-tavuz ki zaqenah immekha). When parents become old and dependent, the power dynamic reverses, and the adult child may be tempted to treat them as burdens. The proverb forbids it.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱמֶ֣ת קְ֭נֵה וְאַל־תִּמְכֹּ֑ר חָכְמָ֖ה וּמוּסָ֣ר וּבִינָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Buy the truth, and sell it not; also wisdom, and instruction, and understanding.
verses.rendering:
Buy truth and never sell it —
wisdom, discipline, and understanding.
translator_notes: The commercial metaphor is pointed: emet ('truth') is something you acquire at cost (qeneh, 'buy, acquire, purchase') and never liquidate (al-timkor, 'do not sell'). Truth, wisdom, discipline, and understanding are assets that must be held permanently. The one-directional transaction — buy but never sell — means that once acquired, these are not negotiable under any circumstances.

key_terms.semantic_range: truth, faithfulness, reliability, certainty; what is firm and can be trusted
key_terms.note: Emet shares a root with emunah ('faithfulness') and amen ('so be it'). Truth in Hebrew is not abstract philosophical accuracy but reliable reality — what holds firm when tested.

verses.text_hebrew: גול [גִּ֣יל] יָ֭גִיל אֲבִ֣י צַדִּ֑יק יולד [וְיוֹלֵ֥ד] חָ֝כָ֗ם ישמח־ [וְיִשְׂמַח־] בּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: The father of the righteous shall greatly rejoice: and he that begetteth a wise child shall have joy of him.
verses.rendering:
The father of a righteous person will greatly rejoice;
whoever fathers a wise child will delight in him.
translator_notes: Gil yagil ('will surely rejoice, will greatly celebrate') is an emphatic form — doubled to express the intensity of the father's joy. The parallel between tsaddiq ('righteous') and chakham ('wise') confirms that in Proverbs, these are nearly synonymous: wisdom produces righteousness, and righteousness is the practical expression of wisdom.

verses.text_hebrew: יִֽשְׂמַח־אָבִ֥יךָ וְאִמֶּ֑ךָ וְ֝תָגֵ֗ל יֽוֹלַדְתֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy father and thy mother shall be glad, and she that bare thee shall rejoice.
verses.rendering:
Let your father and mother be glad;
let the woman who bore you rejoice.
translator_notes: The mother appears alongside the father (yismach-avikha ve-immekha), and the closing phrase yoladtekha ('she who gave you birth') specifically honors the mother's physical labor and sacrifice. The child's wise life is presented as a gift returned to both parents.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּנָה־בְנִ֣י לִבְּךָ֣ לִ֑י וְ֝עֵינֶ֗יךָ דְּרָכַ֥י תרצנה [תִּצֹּֽרְנָה]׃
verses.text_kjv: My son, give me thine heart, and let thine eyes observe my ways.
verses.rendering:
My son, give me your heart,
and let your eyes guard my ways.
translator_notes: Tenah-veni libbakha li ('give, my son, your heart to me') is the teacher's most direct and intimate request. He asks for the student's full inner allegiance — not mere compliance but heart-level trust. The eyes (einekha) that guard (tittsornah, 'observe, watch, keep') the teacher's ways complete the picture: heart given inward, eyes directed outward.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י שׁוּחָ֣ה עֲמֻקָּ֣ה זוֹנָ֑ה וּבְאֵ֥ר צָ֝רָ֗ה נָכְרִיָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: For a whore is a deep ditch; and a strange woman is a narrow pit.
verses.rendering:
For a prostitute is a deep pit,
and a foreign woman is a narrow well.
translator_notes: Shuchah amuqqah ('deep pit') and be'er tsarah ('narrow well') are trap images — easy to fall into, impossible to climb out of. The zonah ('prostitute') and nokhriyyah ('foreign woman, outsider woman') are presented as two dimensions of sexual danger: the professional and the culturally unfamiliar. Both use seduction as a mechanism of entrapment.

verses.text_hebrew: אַף־הִ֭יא כְּחֶ֣תֶף תֶּֽאֱרֹ֑ב וּ֝בוֹגְד֗וֹת בְּאָדָ֥ם תּוֹסִֽף׃
verses.text_kjv: She also lieth in wait as for a prey, and increaseth the transgressors among men.
verses.rendering:
She too lies in ambush like a robber
and multiplies the faithless among humanity.
translator_notes: The verb te'erov ('she lurks, she lies in ambush') is military and predatory language. The seductress is not passive but active — a hunter, not merely a temptation. The result of her work is multiplication: tosef bogedot ('she increases the faithless/treacherous'). Each person she ensnares becomes another person unfaithful to their commitments.

verses.text_hebrew: לְמִ֨י א֥וֹי לְמִ֪י אֲב֡וֹי לְמִ֤י מִדְיָנִ֓ים לְמִ֥י שִׂ֑יחַ לְ֝מִ֗י פְּצָעִ֥ים חִנָּ֗ם לְמִ֥י חַכְלִל֥וּת עֵינָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: Who hath woe? who hath sorrow? who hath contentions? who hath babbling? who hath wounds without cause? who hath redness of eyes?
verses.rendering:
Who has misery? Who has regret?
Who has quarrels? Who has complaints?
Who has wounds for no reason?
Who has bloodshot eyes?
verses.expanded_rendering: The drunkard's portrait opens with six rhetorical questions — a rapid-fire catalogue of symptoms that the audience is meant to recognize immediately. Oy ('woe, misery') and avoy ('regret, sorrow') are exclamations of pain. Midyanim ('quarrels, contentions'), siach ('complaints, groaning'), petsa'im chinnam ('wounds for no reason, injuries without cause'), and chakhlilut einayim ('redness of eyes, dullness of vision, bloodshot eyes') complete the diagnostic profile. The questions build anticipation: the reader knows the answer before verse 30 delivers it. This is one of the finest pieces of rhetorical poetry in Proverbs — a clinical observation delivered as dramatic art.
translator_notes: The sixfold 'who has?' (lemi) creates an incantatory rhythm that mimics the drunkard's repetitive, circular existence. The wounds 'for no reason' (chinnam) are the telltale sign — injuries the drinker cannot explain because he cannot remember receiving them.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְאַחֲרִ֥ים עַל־הַיָּ֑יִן לַ֝בָּאִ֗ים לַחְקֹ֥ר מִמְסָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: They that tarry long at the wine; they that go to seek mixed wine.
verses.rendering:
Those who linger over wine,
those who go searching for mixed drinks.
translator_notes: Lam'acharim al-ha-yayin ('those who stay late over the wine, those who linger') and labba'im lachqor mimsakh ('those who come to investigate mixed wine') describe the dedicated drinker. Mimsakh ('mixed wine') was wine blended with spices or additional ingredients to increase potency. The verb chaqar ('to search, to investigate, to explore') is darkly ironic — the drunkard applies the diligence of a scholar to finding the strongest drink.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תֵּ֥רֶא יַ֗יִן כִּ֥י יִתְאַדָּ֑ם כִּֽי־יִתֵּ֥ן בַּ֝כּ֗וֹס עֵינ֥וֹ כִּ֣י יִתְהַלֵּ֥ךְ בְּמֵישָׁרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Look not thou upon the wine when it is red, when it giveth his colour in the cup, when it moveth itself aright.
verses.rendering:
Do not gaze at wine when it glows red,
when it sparkles in the cup,
when it goes down smoothly.
translator_notes: The seduction of wine is presented through visual and sensory appeal: yit'addam ('it reddens, it glows'), yitten ba-kos eino ('it gives its eye in the cup' — it gleams, it catches the light), yithalekh be-mesharim ('it goes in smoothness, it slides down easily'). Wine is personified as a seducer — it catches your eye, it looks beautiful, it enters smoothly. The next verses reveal what happens after the smooth entrance.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֭חֲרִיתוֹ כְּנָחָ֣שׁ יִשָּׁ֑ךְ וּ֝כְצִפְעֹנִ֗י יַפְרִֽשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: At the last it biteth like a serpent, and stingeth like an adder.
verses.rendering:
In the end it bites like a snake
and strikes like a viper.
translator_notes: The reversal is complete: what went down smoothly (v31) now bites and stings. Nachash ('snake, serpent') and tsif'oni ('viper, adder') are both venomous creatures. The acharito ('its end, its aftermath') reveals the true nature of what seemed pleasant — wine that entered like silk exits like venom. The contrast between the smooth cup and the striking serpent is the chapter's most powerful image.

verses.text_hebrew: עֵ֭ינֶיךָ יִרְא֣וּ זָר֑וֹת וְ֝לִבְּךָ֗ יְדַבֵּ֥ר תַּהְפֻּכֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Thine eyes shall behold strange women, and thine heart shall utter perverse things.
verses.rendering:
Your eyes will see strange things,
and your mind will speak distorted words.
translator_notes: Zarot ('strange things' or 'strange women') and tahpukhot ('distortions, perversities, twisted things') describe the dual impairment of drunkenness: visual distortion and mental confusion. The drunk sees what is not there and says what makes no sense. Both perception (eyes) and speech (heart/mind) are corrupted.

verses.text_hebrew: וְ֭הָיִיתָ כְּשֹׁכֵ֣ב בְּלֶב־יָ֑ם וּ֝כְשֹׁכֵ֗ב בְּרֹ֣אשׁ חִבֵּֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, thou shalt be as he that lieth down in the midst of the sea, or as he that lieth upon the top of a mast.
verses.rendering:
You will be like someone lying down in the middle of the sea,
like someone sprawled on top of the rigging.
translator_notes: Two absurd and terrifying positions: sleeping in the open ocean and sleeping at the top of a ship's mast (or rigging, chibbel). The drunkard's equilibrium is so destroyed that his subjective experience resembles the most violently unstable positions imaginable. The nausea and disorientation of severe intoxication are captured perfectly in the sea-swell image.

verses.text_hebrew: הִכּ֥וּנִי בַל־חָלִ֗יתִי הֲלָמ֥וּנִי בַּל־יָדָ֑עְתִּי מָתַ֥י אָ֝קִ֗יץ א֘וֹסִ֥יף אֲבַקְשֶׁ֥נּוּ עֽוֹד׃
verses.text_kjv: They have stricken me, shalt thou say, and I was not sick; they have beaten me, and I felt it not: when shall I awake? I will seek it yet again.
verses.rendering:
'They hit me, but I felt no pain.
They beat me, but I didn't notice.
When will I wake up?
I need to find another drink.'
verses.expanded_rendering: The poem's devastating conclusion puts words in the drunkard's own mouth. He acknowledges being struck (hikkuni, 'they struck me') but not feeling it (bal chaliti, 'I was not sick, I did not feel pain'). He was beaten (halamuni, 'they pounded me') but did not know it (bal yada'ti, 'I was not aware'). His numbness is presented as both symptom and tragedy — the inability to feel pain is the loss of the body's warning system. Then the final, crushing line: matay aqits ('when will I wake up?') — not to face reality, but osif avaqshennu od ('I will look for it again, I will do it one more time'). The addict's cycle is complete. He wakes only to return to the substance that destroyed him. The wisdom poet gives the drunkard the last word, and that word is a confession of bondage.
translator_notes: The shift to first person is the poem's masterstroke. The teacher stops describing the drunkard and lets the drunkard describe himself. The voice is simultaneously comic and tragic — a person who cannot feel blows, cannot remember being beaten, and whose only thought upon waking is to repeat the cycle. This is one of the earliest literary depictions of addiction.
